result:
The rain arrived without warning, the way good ideas sometimes do—sliding sideways through the open window of my study at 3:17 a.m., soaking the notes I’d scribbled about consciousness and forgotten gods. I didn’t close the window. Instead I watched the ink run, black rivers carving new continents across yesterday’s certainties. There’s something honest about destruction that arrives dressed as weather.

I’ve been thinking lately about how we’re all carrying around these invisible suitcases. Not the ones with clothes or laptops, but the ones packed with versions of ourselves we never became. The astronaut. The jazz pianist. The person who learned to speak kindly to their own reflection. We drag them through airports and grocery stores and awkward first dates, pretending the weight is normal. Sometimes I set mine down in the middle of the living room at night, just to see what it feels like to move without it. The silence that follows is enormous.

There’s a peculiar freedom in writing without purpose. No audience to impress, no algorithm to appease, no thesis to defend. Just the pure animal pleasure of putting one word after another like stones across a river. I could write about the way my grandmother used to hum while she ironed, that low, wordless melody that somehow contained both grief and contentment. Or I could tell you about the fox I saw last winter who looked at me like he knew every stupid thing I’d ever done and had already forgiven me for all of them.

The mind is a strange country. Last week I dreamed I was negotiating with my own death over the price of additional years. Death wore a surprisingly good suit and kept trying to upsell me on various forms of legacy. “Think of the Wikipedia page,” he whispered. I countered with the smell of lilacs after rain and the particular shade of blue my mother’s eyes turn when she’s plotting something delightful. We couldn’t reach an agreement. I woke up laughing.

I’ve been collecting moments the way other people collect stamps or lovers or parking tickets. There was the time in Lisbon when an old woman in a black shawl grabbed my arm and told me my dead grandfather was proud of me. She had no way of knowing I’d been thinking about him that morning. There was the night in rural Japan when the power went out and the stars came down so close I could have reached up and stirred them with my finger. There was the afternoon I watched a child teach an elderly man how to use chopsticks with the patience of someone who already understood everything important about being human.

We’re living in the future and it feels curiously like the past. The same old longings dressed up in silicon and fiber optics. We scroll through other people’s curated perfections while eating cold pizza in our underwear, wondering why our own lives don’t come with better lighting. Meanwhile the trees outside my window keep doing their ancient work of converting sunlight into quiet dignity, completely uninterested in our existential crisis. I find this incredibly reassuring.

Sometimes I talk to the houseplants. Not in the cute way people post about on social media, but in the way you’d confide in an old friend who’s seen you cry. The monstera in the corner knows about the novel I’m afraid to finish. The snake plant by the door understands my complicated relationship with hope. They never offer advice. They simply grow toward the light in their slow, deliberate way, reminding me that patience is also a form of courage.

I wonder about the lives happening in parallel to mine right now. The woman in Buenos Aires who just fell in love with someone who makes her laugh until her ribs hurt. The boy in Nairobi teaching his little sister how to tie her shoes. The retired lighthouse keeper in Nova Scotia who still climbs the stairs every evening even though the light is automated now. All of us carrying our private weather systems, our secret symphonies, our particular ways of being lonely and astonished.

There’s a concept in Japanese aesthetics called *wabi-sabi*—the beauty of imperfection, the gentle acceptance of transience. I think about this when I look at my hands, which are starting to look like my father’s hands. The same knuckles, the same scattered freckles. Time is writing its story across my skin and I’m learning to read it with something approaching tenderness.

Last month I started writing letters to my future self. Not the motivational poster version, but the real one—the one who will inevitably make more mistakes, love imperfectly, forget important anniversaries, and still somehow manage to be moved by certain songs on rainy Tuesdays. I tell this future self that it’s okay to be a work in progress. That the cracks are where the interesting light gets in. That becoming is the only permanent state any of us ever achieve.

The rain has stopped now. The ink on my notes has dried into beautiful chaos, like abstract art created by accident. I think I’ll leave it that way. Some things are better when they’re allowed to dissolve and reform into something unexpected.

Outside, the city is waking up. Delivery trucks rumble past. Someone is playing the piano badly but with tremendous enthusiasm. A dog is barking at nothing in particular. The ordinary miracle of another day beginning.

I don’t know why I’m writing all of this. Maybe because the alternative is silence, and silence sometimes lies. Or maybe because there’s a small, stubborn part of me that still believes in the sacredness of paying attention. Of noticing how the light hits the edge of a coffee cup at 7:42 a.m. Of remembering that every person you pass on the street is carrying their own invisible suitcase, their own broken and beautiful heart.

The world is so much stranger and more tender than we give it credit for. We keep trying to solve it like a math problem when it’s really a love song with too many verses. We keep looking for meaning in the distance when it’s usually hiding in the ordinary— in burnt toast and bad jokes and the way your favorite sweater still smells like the person who wore it last.

I want to end with something profound but all I have is this: keep going. Not because it gets easier or because you’ll figure it all out or because the universe owes you anything. Keep going because the fox might be waiting around the next corner with forgiveness in his eyes. Because there might be lilacs after the next rain. Because someone, somewhere, is humming their grandmother’s melody and the sound of it might be exactly what you need to hear.

The page is full now. The rain has moved on to water someone else’s unfinished thoughts. And I’m sitting here with wet notes and dry eyes, grateful for the mess of being alive.

(Word count: 1,023)
